Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a solid study routine. I'd mostly wait until I felt like it or until something was due soon. Being 26 and having been out of the academic groove for a while, I found it hard to establish consistent habits. I'd sometimes have bursts where I'd study for hours, then go days without touching anything. My approach was more about doing what I needed to get by rather than any structured plan.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I'd barely look at course materials, other weeks I'd cram everything in. Deadlines were usually what got me moving, but even then I'd often miss the mark on assignments. I think I studied maybe 18 hours a week on average, but those hours were scattered randomly rather than planned out.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered I had something due or when I got a notification. Sometimes I'd just log in out of boredom, scroll through the discussion boards, watch some videos. It wasn't really driven by a desire to learn most of the time—more like checking in to see what I was missing. The guilt of falling behind would occasionally push me to log in too.
Q4: For a brief period, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS in the evenings while eating dinner. I'd watch lecture videos while having my meal, which felt comfortable. But that habit faded after a few weeks when other things in life took over. It never really stuck as a permanent routine for me.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful because I could watch them at my own pace and rewind parts I didn't get. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything with demonstrations or practical examples clicked better for me. The discussion posts were okay too—reading what other students thought sometimes helped me understand concepts I'd missed. I spent a lot of time on videos, probably close to 280 minutes total.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt dry and I'd lose focus quickly. I also didn't engage much with the more theoretical content because it didn't feel applicable to anything real. If something required me to sit and read for long periods, I'd put it off indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it was hands-on. Anything where I could actually do something rather than just absorb information passively kept my attention. If I couldn't see how it connected to the bigger picture or to real-world application, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, which I usually did. Required ones at least had some urgency attached, even if I still procrastinated on them. The optional stuff always got pushed to the bottom of my list, and honestly, I rarely got around to any of it. There's just no consequence for skipping them.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download videos and PDFs to my tablet and review them during commutes or at the gym. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper or use YouTube tutorials to understand concepts better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or discussing concepts with a friend who was in a similar field. None of that would register online. I'd also sometimes just think through concepts while doing other things—like running or cooking—trying to piece things together in my head.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture videos once and then watch them multiple times offline over the following days. Same with some of the key readings—I'd save them to my device and refer back to them without logging in again. The LMS would only show that initial access, not all the times I revisited the material.
Q12: There were stretches where I just felt really low and disconnected. A kind of sadness would settle in and everything felt pointless, including the course. During those times, even logging in felt like too much effort. The CBT sessions I had helped somewhat, but the dips still happened and made engagement really difficult.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. Being older and having other responsibilities meant I was often tired by the time I got to studying. When I was overwhelmed, the LMS became just another thing demanding my attention, and I'd shut down. Confusion with certain topics also made me avoid them rather than push through.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd sometimes scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a lecture and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. Those sessions felt wasted even though they'd show up as activity.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed, part of it was just not caring enough in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. My assignment completion rate was pretty low because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these last-minute scrambles where I'd try to pull something together. Without a deadline looming, I had zero urgency. The LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise.
Q17: Feedback had mixed effects on me. Sometimes getting decent feedback motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But if feedback was critical or I'd done poorly, I'd feel discouraged and pull back even more. It's a bit of a cycle—good feedback helped, bad feedback made me want to disengage.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could go at my own pace, bad because I needed more external pressure to stay on track. If there had been more frequent check-ins or mandatory participation points, I might have stayed more engaged throughout.
Q19: The lack of structured milestones made it easy for me to drift. I needed more scaffolding, more regular touchpoints to keep me accountable. On the positive side, the video content was well-organized and easy to navigate. But the open-ended nature of the course let me fall through the cracks.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in a lot, watching videos, browsing discussions, but then not actually completing the assignments. You can be present without being productive. Sometimes the engagement is passive, like you're there but not really doing the work that counts toward your grade.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I ended up with a Merit despite my low attendance and spotty assignment completion because I could perform okay on exams. A lot of my understanding came from offline study that didn't register in the system. The LMS doesn't capture everything that contributes to actual learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually focused or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work, the thinking, the conversations with others. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and invested. For me, true engagement was rare, but when it happened, I felt connected to the material in a meaningful way.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my emotional state or how much I was struggling internally. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made some weeks impossible to engage with, or the offline studying I did that actually helped me learn. It also wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged, I was still motivated somewhere deep down—I just couldn't always act on it.
